h1Title: Chronic Otitis Media: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic otitis media is persistent inflammation of the middle ear lasting 6 weeks or more, affecting the tympanic membrane, ossicular chain, and mastoid air cells. It is the world's most common cause of acquired conductive hearing loss in children, accounting for approximately 164 million cases of chronic otitis media with effusion globally and 31 million new acute otitis media episodes annually in children under 5. The disease spectrum ranges from non-suppurative glue ear with fluid behind an intact eardrum to suppurative disease with tympanic membrane perforation and persistent ear discharge. Untreated or recurring disease risks permanent hearing loss, cholesteatoma, mastoiditis, and intracranial complications. Surgical repair (tympanoplasty, mastoidectomy) restores hearing and eliminates infection in 85-90% of appropriately selected patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 164 million people globally have chronic otitis media with effusion (WHO 2004 global burden study); 1-4% of children in high-income countries have clinically significant hearing loss from middle ear disease
keyStats.demographics: Peak acute episodes in children ages 6-24 months; chronic disease most prevalent in indigenous and low-income populations where recurrence and treatment access differ
keyStats.avgAge: Acute otitis media peaks in children under 5; chronic suppurative disease and surgical need concentrated in ages 3-15 years, though adult residual disease is common
keyStats.globalCases: ~21,000 deaths annually attributable to complications of chronic suppurative otitis media (WHO 2004); mastoiditis and meningitis from untreated disease are the leading causes

definition: Chronic otitis media (ICD-10: H66) is defined as persistent or recurrent inflammation of the middle-ear cleft — comprising the Eustachian tube, middle-ear cavity, ossicular chain, and mastoid air cells — lasting longer than 6 weeks despite initial treatment, or characterized by structural damage to the tympanic membrane. The condition is classified into three main subtypes: chronic otitis media with effusion (COME, 'glue ear'), in which fluid accumulates behind an intact but retracted or non-mobile tympanic membrane causing conductive hearing loss; chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM), characterized by persistent mucopurulent discharge through a tympanic membrane perforation with active mucosal infection; and chronic otitis media with cholesteatoma, where an abnormal accumulation of keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium in the middle ear erodes bone, threatens the ossicular chain, facial nerve, labyrinth, and intracranial structures. All forms are managed by otolaryngologists (ear-nose-throat surgeons). The fundamental underlying cause in most cases is Eustachian tube dysfunction, which prevents normal middle-ear ventilation and drainage.

typesClassification.type: Chronic otitis media with effusion (glue ear)
typesClassification.description: Fluid trapped in the middle ear behind an intact, non-perforated tympanic membrane for 3 or more months. Most common in children aged 1-7 years. Causes conductive hearing loss of 20-40 dB. Most cases resolve spontaneously within 3 months; persistent cases are treated with ventilation tube (grommet) insertion.

Persistent purulent ear discharge for 6 weeks or more through a tympanic membrane perforation without cholesteatoma. Mucosal type (tubotympanic) has a central perforation and typically lower complication risk. Infection driven by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. Treated with topical antibiotics and surgical tympanic membrane repair.

typesClassification.type: Chronic otitis media with cholesteatoma (atticoantral disease)
typesClassification.description: Keratinizing squamous epithelium invades and expands in the middle ear. Aggressive bone erosion can destroy the ossicles, labyrinth, facial nerve canal, tegmen, and sigmoid sinus plate. Requires surgical removal (mastoidectomy) — non-surgical treatment is not curative.

Severe retraction of the tympanic membrane that adheres to the ossicles, promontory, or posterior canal wall, causing significant conductive hearing loss and predisposing to cholesteatoma. May follow recurrent acute otitis media with tube dysfunction.

primarySymptoms: Persistent or intermittent painless ear discharge (otorrhea) lasting more than 6 weeks, typically mucopurulent in suppurative disease, which may have a malodorous quality if cholesteatoma is present.

Conductive hearing loss in the affected ear, ranging from mild (15-20 dB in effusion) to moderate-severe (40-60 dB with ossicular discontinuity), often recognized by the patient as a blocked or muffled ear.
Ear fullness or pressure sensation, particularly in glue ear, where fluid creates a dampened resonance and a plugged feeling that worsens with altitude changes.
Chronic ear pain or deep otalgia is unusual in uncomplicated chronic disease but develops when secondary bacterial infection, mastoid involvement, or cholesteatoma is present.
Tinnitus — ringing, buzzing, or hissing in the affected ear — from fluid, ossicular damage, or sensorineural involvement in long-standing disease.
Recurrent acute exacerbations of discharge and pain superimposed on chronically unhealthy ear, triggered by upper respiratory infections or water entering the ear.
Blocked Eustachian tube sensation with clicking, popping, or crackling sounds when swallowing or yawning, reflecting impaired tube pressure equalization.
Vertigo or balance disturbance if the labyrinth is eroded by cholesteatoma, indicating an inner ear fistula requiring urgent surgical management.
Facial weakness or paralysis from facial nerve compression by cholesteatoma or suppurative infection within the fallopian canal — a surgical emergency.
earlyWarningSigns: Glue ear suspected in a child who does not respond normally to spoken voice, turns up the television volume, or is noted to have language delay — conductive hearing loss from effusion is the leading cause of language delay in early childhood.
Visible eardrum retraction pocket in the postero-superior quadrant on otoscopy — an early indicator of pars flaccida cholesteatoma developing at the point of greatest negative pressure.

Persistent otorrhea for more than 2 weeks despite appropriate antibiotic ear drops — indicates biofilm-forming bacteria (typically Pseudomonas aeruginosa) that will not clear without prolonged or surgical management.
Tympanic membrane perforation found incidentally — even without current discharge, a chronic perforation requires ENT evaluation for hearing assessment and surgical repair planning.
emergencySigns: Facial nerve palsy (drooping of one side of the face) associated with known ear disease — indicates cholesteatoma or acute mastoiditis compressing the facial nerve; requires same-day surgical evaluation.

Severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and fever with otorrhea — intracranial extension of suppurative ear infection causing meningitis or brain abscess; emergency CT and neurosurgical assessment.

Postauricular swelling, erythema, tenderness, and forward displacement of the ear (auricle pushed anteriorly) — acute mastoiditis requiring immediate ENT assessment and IV antibiotics or mastoid surgery.
Sudden onset of vertigo, sensorineural hearing loss, and tinnitus in a patient with known cholesteatoma — labyrinthine fistula requiring urgent surgical exploration.

causes.description: The Eustachian tube equalizes middle ear pressure and drains secretions. In young children, the tube is shorter, more horizontal, and less rigid, facilitating nasopharyngeal reflux of bacteria and impaired drainage. Adenoid hypertrophy, cleft palate, and Down syndrome all impair Eustachian tube function and are the primary risk factors for recurrent and chronic middle ear disease.

Each episode of acute otitis media caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, or Moraxella catarrhalis leaves behind residual mucosal inflammation, effusion, or a weakened tympanic membrane. Three or more episodes within 6 months are defined as recurrent acute otitis media and predict progression to chronic disease in 20-30% of affected children.

In chronic suppurative otitis media, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) establish biofilms on the middle ear mucosa and tympanic membrane that resist standard systemic antibiotic penetration. Topical quinolone ear drops (ciprofloxacin) achieve bactericidal concentrations 100-1,000× higher than systemic dosing and are more effective for biofilm eradication.

Perforation from acute suppurative otitis media, trauma, or failed tympanoplasty provides a direct pathway for water and bacteria to enter the middle ear. Central perforations in the pars tensa are generally less dangerous than marginal or attic perforations, which are associated with ingrowth of keratinizing squamous epithelium forming a cholesteatoma.

Negative middle-ear pressure from chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction creates retraction pockets, most commonly in the pars flaccida (attic) or postero-superior pars tensa. Accumulated keratin debris creates a self-expanding mass that secretes collagenases and prostaglandins, causing progressive bone erosion.

causes.cause: Upper respiratory tract infections and allergic rhinitis
causes.description: Viral URI causes Eustachian tube edema and temporary obstruction, triggering acute exacerbations. Allergic inflammation maintains chronic tube swelling; clinical studies show children with allergic rhinitis have 2-3× higher rates of otitis media with effusion than non-allergic peers.

riskFactors.description: The Eustachian tube matures gradually; immature anatomy is the primary reason for the peak incidence of otitis media between 6 and 24 months. By age 7, most children have outgrown the anatomical predisposition to middle ear disease.

Cleft palate causes structural Eustachian tube dysfunction and nearly 100% incidence of otitis media with effusion requiring ventilation tubes. Down syndrome carries a 60-80% rate of chronic ear disease from Eustachian tube anatomical variance.

Enlarged adenoids obstruct Eustachian tube orifices and act as a bacterial reservoir. Adenoidectomy combined with ventilation tube insertion reduces recurrence risk by 40-60% versus tubes alone in children over 4 years.
Parental smoking doubles the child's risk of otitis media with effusion; smoke impairs ciliary function in the Eustachian tube mucosa and increases nasopharyngeal bacterial carriage.

Daycare children have 2-3× higher rates of respiratory viral infections, which are the primary triggers for acute otitis media episodes. Children in large group daycare settings have significantly higher rates of recurrent otitis media.

Indigenous populations in Australia, Canada, Alaska, and the Pacific have prevalence rates of chronic suppurative otitis media of 5-46%, far exceeding WHO's 4% threshold for identification as a public health problem, driven by housing, nutrition, and access factors.

Breastfeeding for at least 6 months reduces otitis media risk by approximately 23% (meta-analysis 2016) through passive immunity transfer and avoidance of bottle-feeding supine position which promotes Eustachian tube reflux.

affectedDemographics: Children ages 1-7 years: peak incidence of both acute and chronic otitis media with effusion; approximately 80% of children have at least one episode by age 3.
Indigenous children in Australia, New Zealand, Arctic regions, and Pacific islands: 5-46% prevalence of chronic suppurative otitis media, reflecting structural health inequities.
Children and adults with craniofacial anomalies: cleft palate, Down syndrome, and choanal atresia populations have near-universal middle ear disease.
School-age children with hearing loss: in 10-15% of children with unilateral or bilateral hearing loss identified by school screening, chronic otitis media with effusion is the cause.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with pneumatic otoscopy, which is the gold standard for clinical assessment of middle ear fluid and tympanic membrane mobility. A normal tympanic membrane shows light reflex, visible ossicular chain, and full pneumatic mobility; effusion causes a retracted, immobile, or amber-discolored drum. Tympanometry quantifies middle ear pressure and compliance: a flat (type B) tympanogram with reduced ear canal volume confirms effusion; a type C tympanogram indicates negative pressure without effusion. Audiometry — pure-tone audiogram and speech discrimination testing — establishes the degree of hearing loss and guides urgency of intervention; a conductive gap above 25 dB warrants treatment consideration. Otomicroscopy under a surgical microscope is the gold standard for identifying cholesteatoma: white keratinous material in a retraction pocket, typically in the postero-superior quadrant or pars flaccida, is diagnostic. CT of the temporal bones confirms the extent of bone erosion, tegmen integrity, sigmoid sinus plate, and ossicular destruction when cholesteatoma is suspected or surgical planning is required. Audiometric screening in children is crucial; silent glue ear causing 30-40 dB hearing loss is easily missed without structured school or pediatric surveillance.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes tympanic membrane appearance and assesses mobility with gentle pressure; the primary clinical diagnostic tool for middle ear effusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld otoscope with a pneumatic bulb attachment is used by the clinician. The ear canal is sealed and small puffs of air assess drum movement. The procedure takes 1-2 minutes and is painless in most patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures middle ear compliance and pressure; type B (flat) tympanogram indicates effusion; type C indicates negative pressure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small probe is placed at the ear canal entrance, sealing it gently. A tone and pressure sweep take 30-60 seconds per ear. The result is a graph (tympanogram) reviewed immediately. Painless and non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hearing loss by frequency and type (conductive versus sensorineural); conductive loss from middle ear disease shows an air-bone gap
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You sit in a sound-insulated booth wearing headphones and press a button when you hear tones at various frequencies and volumes. Takes 15-30 minutes. Children under 4 use play audiometry or visual reinforcement audiometry.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed examination of the tympanic membrane and ear canal under magnification; required to identify cholesteatoma, perforations, retraction pockets, and granulation tissue
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The ENT surgeon examines the ear using a binocular microscope, occasionally suctioning secretions with a thin instrument. Takes 5-10 minutes in the clinic; may require general anesthetic in young children or uncooperative patients.

diagnosticTests.test: High-resolution CT of the temporal bones
diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps bone erosion, ossicular chain status, tegmen, sigmoid sinus plate, and labyrinthine fistula in suspected or confirmed cholesteatoma; guides surgical approach
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a CT scanner for 10-15 minutes without contrast injection. The scan is painless. Results are reviewed by an ENT surgeon and radiologist to plan surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism in CSOM — commonly Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus — and determines antibiotic sensitivities to guide topical therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton or flocked swab is passed into the discharging ear canal by the clinician. The procedure takes seconds; results return in 48-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment strategy depends on the disease subtype. For chronic otitis media with effusion (glue ear), watchful waiting for 3 months is appropriate for uncomplicated cases because 80% resolve spontaneously. Ventilation tube (grommet, tympanostomy tube) insertion under general anesthetic is indicated when effusion persists beyond 3 months with a hearing loss of 25 dB or more, or when significant language delay, educational impact, or quality of life impairment is documented. The AAO-HNS 2022 clinical practice guideline supports adenoidectomy at the same time as tubes in children over 4 years to reduce recurrence. For chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM), NICE and AAO-HNS guidelines recommend a 2-week course of aural toilet (microsuction of debris) combined with topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% or ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone drops as first-line treatment; topical antibiotics achieve cure rates of 65-80% and are superior to systemic antibiotics alone for CSOM. Tympanoplasty (surgical repair of the tympanic membrane perforation) is performed once the ear is dry for at least 8-12 weeks; success rates for hearing restoration and ear dryness exceed 85% in experienced hands. For cholesteatoma, canal wall-up or canal wall-down mastoidectomy is the definitive treatment; surgery aims to achieve a safe, dry, and hearing-preserving ear. Ossicular chain reconstruction at the time of cholesteatoma surgery restores hearing in 60-75% of cases. All patients benefit from water precautions during active disease and hearing amplification if permanent hearing loss persists.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops (with or without dexamethasone)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic treatment for CSOM. Applied twice daily for 10-14 days after aural toilet to remove debris and biofilm. Ciprofloxacin concentrations in the middle ear from topical application are 100-1,000× systemic levels, overcoming Pseudomonas biofilm resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dry ear rates 65-80% at 4 weeks versus 20-30% with oral antibiotics alone; superiority confirmed in multiple NICE-reviewed RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Aural toilet (microsuction or dry mopping)
medicalTreatments.description: Removal of debris, keratin, and discharge from the ear canal and middle ear using a surgical microscope and suction or cotton wool probes. Essential prerequisite before topical antibiotic drops to improve drug penetration to the middle-ear mucosa.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination of aural toilet plus topical ciprofloxacin achieves cure in 65-80% of CSOM cases within 4 weeks; aural toilet alone inadequate

medicalTreatments.name: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (for acute exacerbations)
medicalTreatments.description: 7-day course for acute-on-chronic otitis media exacerbations with systemic features (fever, mastoid tenderness, cervical lymphadenopathy). Not a substitute for topical treatment in uncomplicated CSOM; systemic antibiotic penetration of middle ear biofilm is inadequate at standard doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens duration of acute exacerbation by 1-2 days; reduces risk of acute mastoiditis in febrile exacerbations

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroid spray (for concurrent allergic rhinitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces Eustachian tube inflammation and improves middle ear ventilation when allergic rhinitis contributes to tube dysfunction. Fluticasone or mometasone nasal spray; a 3-month course is appropriate alongside primary ear treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analysis shows modest 20-25% improvement in glue ear resolution rates when used in conjunction with watchful waiting

medicalTreatments.name: Ventilation tube (grommet) insertion
medicalTreatments.description: Myringotomy with insertion of a small flanged tube into the anteroinferior tympanic membrane under general anesthetic in children; local anesthetic possible in adults. Restores middle ear ventilation immediately; tubes remain in situ 6-18 months. Grommets have transformed management of recurrent acute and chronic secretory otitis media.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hearing improvement of 12 dB (conductive gap closure) in 95% immediately post-insertion; reduces acute ear infection rate by 40-50% during tube function period

surgicalOptions.name: Tympanoplasty (myringoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Repair of tympanic membrane perforation using an autologous graft (temporalis fascia, perichondrium, or fat) placed over or under the perforation margin via a post-auricular or endaural approach. Aims to achieve a dry, intact ear and restore hearing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft uptake and dry ear rates 85-92% in adults; 75-85% in children (re-perforation higher in children due to ongoing Eustachian tube dysfunction)

surgicalOptions.name: Canal wall-up mastoidectomy with tympanoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of mastoid air cells and cholesteatoma while preserving the posterior ear canal wall. Achieves complete cholesteatoma removal in 90-95% of limited disease; second-look surgery at 12-18 months is routinely planned to check for residual disease in the hidden recesses.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Residual cholesteatoma detected at second look in 15-35% when posterior attic disease is present; overall disease control at 5 years 85-90% with second-look strategy

surgicalOptions.name: Canal wall-down mastoidectomy (modified radical mastoidectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Removes posterior canal wall to create an open mastoid bowl, providing the widest access and lowest residual cholesteatoma rate. Requires lifelong annual ear cleaning (aural toilet) by an ENT surgeon. Used for advanced or recurrent cholesteatoma.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Residual disease rates below 10%; mastoid cavity stable without further surgery in 85% of patients long-term

surgicalOptions.name: Ossiculoplasty (ossicular chain reconstruction)
surgicalOptions.description: Reconstruction of eroded ossicles using titanium prostheses (total or partial ossicular replacement prostheses, TORP/PORP) or autologous cortical bone. May be performed simultaneously with cholesteatoma clearance or as a staged second procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hearing restoration (air-bone gap closure to within 20 dB) in 60-75% of cases; results better for partial reconstruction (PORP) than total (TORP)

alternativeTreatments.name: Autoinflation with Valsalva or Otovent device
alternativeTreatments.description: Nasal balloon autoinflation 3 times daily for 1-3 months improves Eustachian tube function and middle ear ventilation in glue ear. The ATTRACT trial (2015, PMID 26606981) showed a 50% relative improvement in tympanometry normalization at 1 month versus control in children; currently NICE-recommended as a first-line non-surgical option.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist (ENT surgeon) should evaluate any child or adult with ear discharge lasting more than 2 weeks, conductive hearing loss not explained by wax, a visible tympanic membrane perforation, or any suspicion of cholesteatoma. Audiological assessment by a specialist audiologist is essential to quantify hearing loss and guide rehabilitation decisions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an ENT surgeon with specific expertise in otology and middle ear surgery. Otologists (ENT surgeons with subspecialty otology training) perform the best outcomes for complex tympanoplasty and cholesteatoma surgery. For pediatric patients, a pediatric otolaryngologist experienced in ventilation tube insertion and adenoidectomy is ideal.
hospitalCriteria: Operating microscope and endoscopic ear surgery capability
Pediatric anesthesia service for grommet insertion in young children
Intraoperative neuromonitoring for facial nerve during mastoid surgery
Audiology department offering diagnostic audiometry and hearing aid fitting
keyFacilities: ENT outpatient clinic with otomicroscope and aural toilet service
Day-surgery theater for ventilation tube insertion
Tertiary otology unit for cholesteatoma and complex middle ear reconstruction
Pediatric audiology service for school-age hearing assessment
preventionStrategies: Ensure infants receive pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13/PCV15) and Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccine; PCV vaccination reduces acute otitis media episodes by 20-25% and prevents the most common bacterial causes.
Breastfeed exclusively for at least 6 months; breastfed infants have a 23% lower risk of otitis media compared with formula-fed peers.
Eliminate tobacco smoke exposure in the home — passive smoke doubles the risk of chronic otitis media with effusion.
Keep water out of ears with a known perforation; use earplugs or cotton wool with petroleum jelly while swimming or bathing.
Promptly treat allergic rhinitis to reduce chronic Eustachian tube inflammation, which perpetuates middle ear disease.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid swimming, diving, and water sports until perforation is surgically repaired; water entry through a perforation triggers bacterial infection and discharge flares.
Complete all prescribed topical antibiotic courses fully — partial courses allow biofilm to re-establish and selection of resistant organisms.
Attend follow-up ENT appointments and audiometry even when ears feel dry — cholesteatoma can be asymptomatic and grow silently between visits.
Fitted hearing aids in children with persistent conductive hearing loss while awaiting surgery or in those not surgical candidates preserve speech and language development.
Practice regular nasal hygiene (saline rinses, treating allergic rhinitis) to reduce the bacterial reservoir in the nasopharynx that seeds the Eustachian tube.

recommended: Vitamin D-rich foods (fatty fish, fortified milk, eggs) and adequate zinc (meat, legumes, pumpkin seeds) to support innate immune function and reduce infection frequency
Anti-inflammatory foods (olive oil, berries, leafy greens) for general mucosal health and reduction of Eustachian tube inflammation
Adequate hydration to maintain thin mucosal secretions that drain more readily from the middle ear
avoid: Bottle feeding while lying flat in infants, which promotes Eustachian tube reflux of nasopharyngeal secretions and is strongly associated with recurrent otitis media
Passive tobacco smoke exposure, which impairs mucociliary clearance and promotes Eustachian tube dysfunction
Known allergens in food-allergic children if allergic rhinitis is contributing to Eustachian tube dysfunction

exerciseGuidelines: Standard physical activity is safe and encouraged for patients with chronic otitis media. Swimming requires ear protection (earplugs with petroleum jelly) in all patients with perforations or ventilation tubes. Diving below the surface is contraindicated with tympanic membrane perforations or ventilation tubes. Hearing-related activity restrictions (e.g., group activities requiring normal hearing) should be lifted as soon as hearing is restored via surgical or audiological intervention.
dailyManagement: Instill prescribed topical antibiotic ear drops twice daily, lying with the affected ear uppermost for 5 minutes after each instillation to allow penetration.
Keep the ear dry: cotton wool with petroleum jelly during bathing; custom silicone earplugs for swimming.
Report any sudden new vertigo, facial weakness, fever, or significant increase in pain promptly — these may indicate cholesteatoma extension or intracranial complications.
Attend annual ENT follow-up for otomicroscopy even during quiescent phases to identify recurrence of cholesteatoma or re-perforation after repair.
In children, ensure annual hearing tests during active disease periods to document any worsening that affects language and school performance.
prognosis: Prognosis depends strongly on subtype and timeliness of management. Chronic otitis media with effusion resolves spontaneously in 80% of children within 3 months, and in those requiring ventilation tubes, recurrence after tube extrusion occurs in 20-30%. Chronic suppurative otitis media treated with appropriate topical antibiotics achieves ear dryness in 65-80% of cases; surgical tympanoplasty achieves a dry, intact ear in 85-92% of adults with success rates somewhat lower in children due to Eustachian tube immaturity. Cholesteatoma has a 15-35% residual disease rate at second-look surgery; long-term disease control at 5 years is 85-90% with adequate surgical clearance. Sensorineural hearing loss from labyrinthine erosion by cholesteatoma is permanent and cannot be restored surgically. Untreated chronic ear disease in children is associated with 5-10 dB permanent conductive hearing loss per year of delay and increasing risk of intracranial complications, which carry 5-15% mortality in high-income countries.
recoveryTimeline: Topical antibiotic treatment for CSOM achieves ear dryness within 2-4 weeks in responsive cases. Post-tympanoplasty, eardrum healing takes 6-12 weeks; hearing improvement is typically apparent at the 6-week audiogram. Mastoidectomy recovery takes 4-6 weeks for wound healing; formal hearing rehabilitation is assessed at 3 months. Children require 6-12 months follow-up after ventilation tube insertion to monitor tube function and hearing.
complications: Permanent conductive hearing loss from ossicular erosion by cholesteatoma or granulation tissue — present in 40-60% of CSOM cases; audiometry quantifies severity before surgery.
Cholesteatoma expansion with labyrinthine erosion causing permanent sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo — an irreversible complication requiring urgent mastoid surgery.
Acute mastoiditis from extension of middle-ear infection into mastoid air cells; presents with postauricular swelling, fever, and marked otalgia; requires IV antibiotics and often surgical drainage.
Intracranial extension: meningitis, epidural abscess, brain abscess, or sigmoid sinus thrombophlebitis from complicated CSOM or cholesteatoma; mortality 5-15% even with treatment.
Facial nerve palsy from cholesteatoma or acute suppurative infection eroding the fallopian canal — requires emergency surgical decompression.
Tympanosclerosis — calcification of the tympanic membrane and middle ear mucosa from recurrent inflammation — causing mild to moderate conductive hearing loss that may require ossiculoplasty.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Guideline NG91 — Otitis media (with effusion) in under 12s
supportResources.description: UK evidence-based clinical guideline covering diagnosis, watchful waiting, ventilation tubes, and hearing management for glue ear.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Head and Neck Surgery: Otitis Media Resources
supportResources.description: Patient resources, clinical practice guidelines, and specialist finder for all forms of otitis media from the US ENT professional society.

supportResources.name: Hear the World Foundation
supportResources.description: International advocacy organization for hearing health in children, providing resources on hearing loss from middle ear disease and access to audiological care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cholesteatoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cholesteatoma is a specific subtype of chronic otitis media defined by abnormal keratin-producing epithelium in the middle ear with bone-destructive properties. Not all chronic otitis media has cholesteatoma; CSOM without cholesteatoma (tubotympanic disease) is managed medically and by tympanoplasty, whereas cholesteatoma always requires surgical removal. The distinction is made by otomicroscopy and CT temporal bone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sensorineural hearing loss originates from the cochlea or auditory nerve, causing a permanent air-bone gap absence on audiometry. Chronic otitis media produces conductive hearing loss with an intact sensorineural component; air-bone gap is the key audiometric finding. Otoscopy and tympanometry are normal in sensorineural loss but abnormal in middle ear disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinnitus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinnitus is a symptom of perceived sound without external stimulus; it can occur with middle ear disease but more commonly arises from sensorineural cochlear or auditory nerve pathology. When tinnitus accompanies hearing loss with abnormal otoscopy or tympanometry, middle ear disease is the working diagnosis; normal findings shift suspicion toward inner ear pathology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A deviated septum impairs nasal airflow and drainage, contributing to Eustachian tube dysfunction and recurrent otitis media, but does not directly cause ear disease. It is a contributing risk factor, not a cause of chronic otitis media; management of the septum may improve Eustachian tube function but does not replace ear-specific treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Cholesteatoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The most serious complication of untreated chronic otitis media; bone-destructive keratinizing cyst requiring mandatory surgical removal.

relatedConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-standing middle ear disease can cause sensorineural hearing loss through labyrinthine fistula, noise from tympanic membrane scarring, or direct cochlear inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Tinnitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Middle ear disease from effusion, perforation, or ossicular damage causes low-frequency conductive tinnitus that typically resolves after surgical correction.

relatedConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
relatedConditions.relevance: Structural nasal obstruction impairs Eustachian tube ventilation and promotes middle ear effusion; septoplasty may form part of ENT management of recurrent ear disease.

faqs.question: What is chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: Chronic otitis media is persistent inflammation of the middle ear lasting 6 weeks or more. It ranges from glue ear — fluid trapped behind an intact eardrum — to suppurative disease with a hole in the eardrum and ongoing discharge. It is the most common cause of conductive hearing loss in children worldwide.

faqs.question: What causes chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: The root cause is Eustachian tube dysfunction, which prevents middle ear ventilation and drainage. This predisposes to fluid accumulation, infection, and tympanic membrane damage. Recurrent ear infections in childhood, adenoid hypertrophy, cleft palate, passive smoking, and daycare attendance are the main risk factors.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms are persistent ear discharge, muffled or reduced hearing, a blocked ear feeling, tinnitus, and ear fullness. Pain is unusual in uncomplicated chronic disease. Sudden vertigo, facial weakness, or severe headache with discharge are warning signs of serious complications requiring emergency evaluation.

faqs.question: Is chronic otitis media serious?
faqs.answer: Untreated chronic otitis media can cause permanent hearing loss, cholesteatoma (bone-eroding ear cyst), mastoiditis, facial nerve palsy, meningitis, and brain abscess. Most cases are manageable with topical antibiotics and surgery, but delays in treatment increase complication risk substantially.

faqs.question: How is chronic otitis media treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on type. Glue ear is managed with watchful waiting, autoinflation, or ventilation tubes (grommets). Suppurative disease is treated with aural toilet (ear cleaning) and topical ciprofloxacin drops, achieving a dry ear in 65-80% of cases. Tympanic membrane holes are repaired surgically (tympanoplasty). Cholesteatoma requires mastoid surgery.

faqs.question: What is a grommet (ventilation tube)?
faqs.answer: A grommet is a tiny plastic or titanium tube inserted into a small slit in the eardrum to ventilate the middle ear and allow fluid to drain. It is placed under general anesthetic in children and restores hearing within days. Most grommets extrude spontaneously after 6-18 months as the eardrum heals around them.

faqs.question: What is glue ear?
faqs.answer: Glue ear (chronic otitis media with effusion) is thick, sticky fluid in the middle ear behind an intact eardrum. It causes conductive hearing loss of 20-40 dB, which can affect language development in children. About 80% of cases resolve within 3 months without treatment; persistent cases require ventilation tubes.

faqs.question: Does chronic otitis media cause permanent hearing loss?
faqs.answer: Chronic otitis media with effusion causes temporary conductive hearing loss that usually resolves with treatment. Long-standing perforations, ossicular erosion by cholesteatoma, or labyrinthine fistula can cause permanent conductive or sensorineural hearing loss. Early surgical repair typically restores hearing to near-normal levels in uncomplicated cases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: Acute otitis media is a rapid-onset, painful ear infection that resolves within 2-3 weeks with or without antibiotics. Chronic otitis media persists beyond 6 weeks, causes structural changes (perforation, effusion, cholesteatoma), and requires specialist assessment. Recurrent acute episodes are a major risk factor for chronic disease.

faqs.question: Can adults get chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: Yes. While most common in children under 7, adults can develop chronic otitis media from untreated childhood disease, Eustachian tube dysfunction, nasopharyngeal tumors, or a perforated eardrum from trauma. Adults with chronic ear discharge, muffled hearing, or recurrent infections should be evaluated by an ENT surgeon.

faqs.question: What is cholesteatoma and how does it relate to ear infections?
faqs.answer: Cholesteatoma is a cyst made of abnormal skin cells that grows in the middle ear behind the eardrum, usually from untreated chronic ear disease. It erodes bone and can destroy the hearing bones, inner ear, facial nerve, or spread to the brain. All cholesteatomas require surgical removal as they never resolve with antibiotics alone.

faqs.question: Are ear drops better than antibiotics for chronic ear infections?
faqs.answer: Yes. Topical ciprofloxacin ear drops are significantly more effective than oral antibiotics for chronic suppurative otitis media. The local concentration from drops is 100-1,000 times higher than systemic levels, overcoming the antibiotic-resistant biofilm that causes chronic infection. Oral antibiotics are reserved for acute exacerbations with systemic features.

faqs.question: Can children with grommets swim?
faqs.answer: Surface swimming with grommets is generally safe for most children; studies show that routine shallow swimming does not significantly increase infection risk. Diving below the surface or swimming in water of uncertain cleanliness (lakes, rivers) increases risk and should be avoided. ENT guidance varies; some surgeons recommend custom earplugs.

faqs.question: How successful is tympanoplasty for a perforated eardrum?
faqs.answer: Tympanoplasty achieves a dry, intact eardrum in 85-92% of adults with a chronic perforation. Hearing typically improves by 10-20 dB at the 6-week audiogram. Success rates are slightly lower in children (75-85%) because of ongoing Eustachian tube immaturity. The surgery is performed under general anesthetic and takes 1-2 hours.

faqs.question: How is mastoiditis related to chronic otitis media?
faqs.answer: Mastoiditis is extension of middle ear infection into the adjacent mastoid air cells and bone, presenting as swelling and tenderness behind the ear with fever and forward displacement of the auricle. It requires IV antibiotics and often surgical drainage (mastoidectomy). It is a complication of untreated or undertreated chronic suppurative otitis media.

simpleName: Chronic ear infection (glue ear, perforated eardrum, cholesteatoma)
metaTitle: Chronic Otitis Media: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Chronic otitis media: ear discharge and hearing loss lasting 6+ weeks. Topical antibiotics, grommet insertion, tympanoplasty, and mastoid surgery reviewed.
